content: Hey Saturday, quick update before my session with Dr. Aisha Rahman tomorrow. My anxiety spikes most around work deadlines and crowded spaces like the subway at 8:30 a.m., and I’ve been feeling on edge again this week.

Thanks for sharing that—those are really clear triggers to bring to Dr. Rahman. Before your session, try a brief thought record for one deadline moment and one crowded-space moment: situation, automatic thought, feeling intensity, and a more balanced thought. Even doing two entries can make the patterns easier to discuss.

I did one thought record on Tuesday (March 12) after my manager asked for revisions by 5 p.m. My automatic thought was “I’m going to mess this up and get fired,” anxiety was like 8/10. Balanced thought was “I’ve met tight deadlines before; I can ask Priya for help if needed,” and it dropped to 5/10.

That’s excellent CBT work—you identified a catastrophic thought and shifted it with evidence. A drop from 8/10 to 5/10 is meaningful progress. Bring that exact example to Dr. Rahman; it shows the technique is working in real time.

She also told me to do behavioral activation, so I scheduled two pleasant things this week: a 20-minute walk in Riverside Park on Wednesday and coffee with Marco on Friday at 6. I almost canceled both, but I went.

That’s a big win, especially since you went despite the urge to cancel. Keep scheduling small, specific activities with time and place, just like you did. It directly counters avoidance and builds momentum.
I’ve also done the 10-minute mindfulness most mornings—about 5 out of 7 days, usually at 7:15 a.m. I still get anxious in crowded trains, but I noticed I’m avoiding less than last month.

5 out of 7 is strong consistency, and reduced avoidance is a key progress marker. For crowded trains, try pairing mindfulness with a coping statement like “This is uncomfortable, not dangerous” while doing slow exhale breathing. You’ve got solid data to review with Dr. Aisha Rahman tomorrow.
